title: Stash, Cherry-pick and Tags
learningObjectives: Use git stash to shelve and restore work-in-progress changes
Apply specific commits from any branch using cherry-pick
Create both lightweight and annotated tags for version marking
Use git describe to generate version strings relative to the nearest tag

theory.content:
Git Stash

The stash is a stack of shelved changes. It lets you save your dirty working directory — modified tracked files and optionally untracked files — so you can switch context without committing incomplete work.

# Save current changes with a descriptive message
git stash push -m "WIP: half-finished auth refactor"

# Shorthand (no message)
git stash

# Include untracked files in the stash
git stash push -u  # or --include-untracked

# List all stash entries
git stash list
# stash@{0}: On main: WIP: half-finished auth refactor
# stash@{1}: On feature/ui: saving UI tweaks

# Apply the top stash and remove it from the stack
git stash pop

# Apply a specific entry without removing it
git stash apply stash@{1}

# Remove a stash entry
git stash drop stash@{1}

# Inspect the diff of a stash entry
git stash show -p stash@{0}

# Create a new branch from a stash entry
git stash branch new-branch stash@{0}

Stash entries are stored in the repo indefinitely until you drop or pop them. Use descriptive messages with -m so you can identify them in git stash list.

Cherry-pick

git cherry-pick SHA applies the diff introduced by a specific commit onto HEAD as a brand-new commit. The new commit gets a new SHA; the original is untouched.

# Apply a single commit onto the current branch
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Apply a range of commits (SHA1 exclusive, SHA2 inclusive)
git cherry-pick SHA1..SHA2

# Stage the changes without creating a commit yet
git cherry-pick --no-commit abc1234

# After a conflict, continue the cherry-pick
git cherry-pick --continue

# Abort a cherry-pick in progress
git cherry-pick --abort

Common use cases: backporting a bug fix from main to a release branch; salvaging a commit accidentally made on the wrong branch.

Tags

Tags mark specific commits — typically release points. Git has two kinds:

  • Lightweight tag: git tag v1.0.0 — just a named pointer (ref) to a commit. No extra metadata.
  • Annotated tag: git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release v1.0.0" — a full Git object storing the tagger's name, email, date, and message. Can be GPG-signed. Best practice for releases.
# Create a lightweight tag at HEAD
git tag v1.0.0

# Create an annotated tag
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release v1.0.0 — first stable release"

# Tag a specific past commit
git tag -a v0.9.0 -m "Release v0.9.0" 5f3a8b2

# List tags matching a pattern
git tag -l "v1.*"

# Push a single tag to origin
git push origin v1.0.0

# Push all local tags to origin
git push origin --tags

# Delete a local tag
git tag -d v1.0.0

# Delete a remote tag
git push origin --delete v1.0.0

# Describe current commit relative to the nearest tag
git describe --tags
# v1.0.0-4-g7f3e1a2  (4 commits after v1.0.0, at hash 7f3e1a2)

Semantic Versioning with Tags

The convention for software releases is MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH (e.g., v2.3.1):

  • MAJOR — breaking/incompatible API changes
  • MINOR — new backward-compatible features
  • PATCH — backward-compatible bug fixes

Pre-release versions: v1.0.0-rc.1, v1.0.0-beta.2. Once a tag is pushed to a shared remote, treat it as immutable — do not move or delete it, as other developers and CI pipelines depend on it.
